articles.headline: National Panchayati Raj Day 2025: Focus on Digital Transformation and Women's Leadership
articles.explainer:
• India celebrates National Panchayati Raj Day, commemorating the 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act coming into force on April 24, 1993.
• This year's theme emphasizes 'Digital Panchayats: Empowering Rural India' and enhancing women's participation in local governance.
• Government highlights progress in e-governance initiatives like eGramSwaraj and online services, aiming for greater transparency and efficiency.
• Discussions focus on challenges such as digital literacy gaps, infrastructure availability, and financial autonomy for PRIs.
• Emphasis on strengthening the role of women elected representatives and promoting grassroots leadership for inclusive development.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States
Devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
Role of women and women's organization
articles.source: Ministry of Panchayati Raj / The Hindu
prelimsPoints: National Panchayati Raj Day is observed annually on April 24.
The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992, came into force on April 24, 1993.
The Act added Part IX 'The Panchayats' to the Constitution.
It mandates the establishment of a three-tier Panchayati Raj system in states with a population exceeding 20 lakh.
Article 243D provides for reservation of seats for SCs, STs, and not less than one-third for women.
eGramSwaraj is a simplified work-based accounting application for Panchayati Raj.
First Panchayati Raj system was inaugurated in Nagaur, Rajasthan, on Oct 2, 1959.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the impact of the 73rd Amendment on democratic decentralization in India, focusing on its successes and persistent challenges.
Discuss how digital initiatives like eGramSwaraj can enhance transparency and efficiency in Panchayati Raj Institutions, while also addressing the digital divide in rural areas.
Analyze the role of women in local governance and suggest measures to further strengthen their leadership and decision-making capacity within PRIs.
Examine the financial autonomy of Panchayats and the recommendations of State Finance Commissions in strengthening grassroots democracy.
Discuss the significance of Gram Sabha in fostering participatory democracy and how its effectiveness can be improved.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting the three-tier Panchayati Raj system with digital elements and women leaders.
articles.trivia: The concept of 'Gram Swaraj' was envisioned by Mahatma Gandhi, emphasizing self-sufficient village republics.

articles.headline: India's Q4 GDP Growth Projections and FY25 Economic Outlook
articles.explainer:
• Economic analysts and various agencies release their projections for India's Q4 (Jan-Mar) GDP growth, indicating robust performance driven by manufacturing and services.
• Government sources express optimism for the full fiscal year 2024-25, citing stable domestic demand, capital expenditure push, and moderating inflation.
• Key challenges identified include geopolitical uncertainties, global economic slowdown, and potential impact of El Niño on agricultural output.
• Discussions around monetary policy stance by RBI, given the inflation trajectory and global interest rate environment.
• Focus on sustained structural reforms to maintain long-term growth momentum and attract foreign investment.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Effects of liberalization on the economy
articles.source: Economic Times / RBI
prelimsPoints: GDP measures the total value of goods and services produced within a country's borders in a specific period.
RBI's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) sets the benchmark interest rates.
Inflation is primarily measured by CPI (retail inflation) in India.
Fiscal year in India runs from April 1 to March 31.
Capital expenditure (CapEx) by the government is a key driver of economic growth.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to India's recent economic growth and discuss the sustainability of this trajectory amidst global headwinds.
Examine the role of monetary and fiscal policies in managing inflation and stimulating economic growth in the current scenario.
Discuss the challenges posed by global economic slowdown and geopolitical uncertainties on India's growth prospects and trade.
Evaluate the impact of government's capital expenditure push on crowding in private investment and creating employment opportunities.
Suggest structural reforms necessary to ensure inclusive and sustainable economic development for India in the long run.
articles.imageDescription: A graph showing India's GDP growth trend and projections.
articles.trivia: India is projected to become the world's third-largest economy by 2027.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Bench to Hear Petitions on Federal Fiscal Relations and Resource Devolution
articles.explainer:
• A Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court is set to hear crucial petitions concerning the principles of federal fiscal relations and the devolution of resources between the Union and states.
• The petitions challenge certain aspects of the Goods and Services Tax (GST) framework and the methodology of financial transfers.
• States argue for greater fiscal autonomy and a larger share of central taxes, citing increasing expenditure responsibilities.
• The Union government emphasizes the need for a unified economic framework and equitable distribution across states.
• The judgment is expected to have significant implications for cooperative federalism and the financial health of states.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States
Issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure
Devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
articles.source: Live Law / The Hindu
prelimsPoints: GST is a destination-based consumption tax.
The GST Council is a constitutional body (Article 279A) that makes recommendations on GST issues.
The Finance Commission (Article 280) recommends the distribution of tax revenues between the Union and states.
Cooperative federalism emphasizes cooperation between different levels of government.
Article 268 to 281 in Part XII of the Constitution deals with financial relations between Centre and states.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving nature of fiscal federalism in India, particularly in the post-GST era, and the challenges faced by states in maintaining fiscal autonomy.
Analyze the role of the Supreme Court in adjudicating disputes related to Centre-State financial relations and its impact on cooperative federalism.
Critically evaluate the recommendations of the latest Finance Commission regarding vertical and horizontal devolution of resources.
Examine the arguments for and against greater fiscal autonomy for states, considering both economic efficiency and equity.
Suggest reforms to strengthen the federal fiscal framework in India, ensuring both stability for the Union and adequate resources for states.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of the Supreme Court building with scales representing federal balance.
articles.trivia: India's federal system is often described as 'quasi-federal' due to a strong central bias.

articles.headline: National Green Hydrogen Mission: India Accelerates Towards Production Targets
articles.explainer:
• India provides an update on the progress of its National Green Hydrogen Mission, emphasizing advancements in production capacity and policy incentives.
• The mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, contributing to energy security and decarbonization.
• Government highlights investments in R&D, pilot projects, and collaborations with international partners to drive technological innovation.
• Challenges include high production costs, establishing robust supply chains, and developing adequate infrastructure for storage and transportation.
• Policy measures like production-linked incentives (PLI) and demand creation mechanisms are crucial for scaling up the industry.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
articles.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy / Business Standard
prelimsPoints: Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water using renewable electricity.
The National Green Hydrogen Mission was approved in January 2023.
Its target is to achieve a green hydrogen production capacity of at least 5 Million Metric Tonnes (MMT) per annum by 2030.
The mission aims to reduce fossil fuel imports and decarbonize industrial sectors.
Electrolysis is the key process for green hydrogen production.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of the National Green Hydrogen Mission for India's energy security and climate change commitments.
Analyze the technological and economic challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production in India and propose solutions.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current policy incentives, such as PLI schemes, in promoting indigenous manufacturing and adoption of green hydrogen technologies.
Examine the potential of green hydrogen as a fuel for various sectors like transport, industry, and power generation in India.
Suggest measures for developing a robust green hydrogen ecosystem, including infrastructure, R&D, and international collaborations.
articles.imageDescription: An illustration of a green hydrogen production plant with wind turbines and solar panels.
articles.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe.

articles.headline: ISRO Provides Critical Updates on Gaganyaan Mission Preparations
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) releases a status update on the Gaganyaan mission, India's maiden human spaceflight program.
• Focus on the progress of crew module development, environmental control and life support systems, and astronaut training.
• Successful completion of key ground tests and preliminary flight demonstrations for various sub-systems is highlighted.
• Challenges include ensuring astronaut safety, perfecting re-entry and recovery procedures, and integrating complex systems.
• The mission aims to demonstrate India's capability for human spaceflight and contribute to scientific research in microgravity.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Awareness in the fields of Space
articles.source: ISRO / The Indian Express
keyTerms: Environmental Control and Life Support System (ECLSS)
prelimsPoints: Gaganyaan is India's first human spaceflight mission.
ISRO is the primary agency responsible for the mission.
The mission aims to send a three-member crew to an orbit of 400 km for a 3-day mission.
GSLV Mk III (LVM3) is the launch vehicle identified for Gaganyaan.
Vyommitra is the humanoid robot being developed for the uncrewed test flights.
Astronauts are being trained in India and with international partners.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic significance of the Gaganyaan mission for India's space capabilities and its geopolitical implications.
Analyze the technological challenges involved in human spaceflight, particularly concerning crew safety and life support systems, and how ISRO is addressing them.
Evaluate the potential scientific and economic benefits that India can derive from undertaking human space missions.
Examine the role of international collaboration in advancing India's human spaceflight program and other space exploration endeavors.
Discuss the ethical considerations and long-term vision for India's manned space exploration efforts.
articles.imageDescription: An artist's impression of the Gaganyaan crew module orbiting Earth.
articles.trivia: If successful, India will be the fourth nation to launch a human spaceflight mission.

articles.headline: India-UK Free Trade Agreement Negotiations Enter Final Stages
articles.explainer:
• Negotiations for the India-UK Free Trade Agreement (FTA) are reported to be in their final stages, with both sides optimistic about an imminent conclusion.
• The FTA aims to significantly boost bilateral trade in goods and services, reduce tariffs, and open market access for businesses.
• Key sticking points reportedly include intellectual property rights, rules of origin, and services sector liberalization.
• A successful FTA is expected to enhance economic cooperation, create jobs, and foster greater investment flows between the two nations.
• The agreement is seen as a crucial step for India's 'Act East' policy and the UK's post-Brexit trade strategy.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India's interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India's interests, Indian diaspora.
articles.source: Ministry of Commerce and Industry / Reuters
prelimsPoints: An FTA is a pact between two or more countries to reduce barriers to imports and exports among them.
India is negotiating FTAs with several countries/blocs, including the UK, EU, and Canada.
The UK is a significant trading partner for India, especially after Brexit.
Key sectors of interest for India include textiles, pharmaceuticals, and IT services.
Services constitute a large portion of bilateral trade between India and the UK.
mainsPoints: Analyze the potential economic benefits and challenges for India arising from a comprehensive Free Trade Agreement with the United Kingdom.
Discuss how the India-UK FTA aligns with India's broader trade policy objectives and its 'Act East' strategy.
Examine the key areas of contention in FTA negotiations and how they reflect the differing economic priorities of India and the UK.
Evaluate the impact of such FTAs on domestic industries and suggest measures to mitigate any adverse effects.
Discuss the role of trade agreements in strengthening India's position in the global economic order.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and the United Kingdom superimposed over a handshake icon.
articles.trivia: India is the world's fifth-largest economy, and the UK is the sixth-largest.

articles.headline: India Boosts Coastal Security with Integrated Surveillance and Patrolling
articles.explainer:
• The Indian government announces new measures to enhance coastal security, integrating advanced surveillance systems and increasing joint patrolling operations.
• Focus on safeguarding maritime borders, preventing infiltration, countering illicit trade, and protecting critical offshore assets.
• Collaboration between the Indian Navy, Coast Guard, Marine Police, and other stakeholders is being strengthened for a multi-layered defence.
• Challenges include vast coastline, numerous islands, traditional fishing communities, and evolving maritime threats from state and non-state actors.
• Investment in modernizing patrol vessels, radar networks, and real-time information sharing platforms is underway.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas
Linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
articles.source: Ministry of Defence / PIB
prelimsPoints: India has a coastline of approximately 7,516.6 km.
The Indian Navy is responsible for overall maritime security, while the Indian Coast Guard focuses on coastal security.
The Marine Police operate under state governments.
The 26/11 Mumbai attacks highlighted vulnerabilities in coastal security.
IMAC in Gurugram acts as the nodal center for coastal security information sharing.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multifaceted challenges to India's coastal security and discuss the strategies adopted by the government to address them.
Evaluate the effectiveness of inter-agency coordination (Navy, Coast Guard, Marine Police) in ensuring a robust coastal defence mechanism.
Discuss the role of technology, such as integrated surveillance systems and real-time data sharing, in enhancing maritime domain awareness.
Examine the socio-economic dimensions of coastal security, particularly concerning the traditional fishing communities and their role as 'eyes and ears'.
Suggest further reforms and investments required to create an impregnable coastal security apparatus for India.
articles.imageDescription: A Coast Guard patrol vessel monitoring the Indian coastline.
articles.trivia: India's territorial waters extend up to 12 nautical miles from the baseline.

articles.headline: National Education Policy 2020: Review of Implementation Progress and Future Roadmap
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education conducts a comprehensive review of the implementation progress of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 across states and institutions.
• Highlights include advancements in curriculum reform, vocational education integration, and digital learning initiatives.
• Challenges persist in teacher training, infrastructure development, equitable access, and ensuring smooth transition across different education levels.
• Discussions focus on accelerating the adoption of NEP recommendations, particularly in higher education and early childhood care.
• Emphasis on stakeholder collaboration, including state governments, educational institutions, and civil society, for successful policy execution.
gsSubTopics: Development processes and the development industry —the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
articles.source: Ministry of Education / Press Information Bureau
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education.
It proposes a new 5+3+3+4 curricular structure.
Focus on multidisciplinary education and flexibility in course choices.
The policy aims for universalization of Early Childhood Care and Education (ECCE) by 2030.
It recommends increasing public investment in education to 6% of GDP.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the transformational potential of the National Education Policy 2020 in addressing the challenges of India's education system.
Discuss the key reforms proposed by NEP 2020 in school and higher education, and analyze their implementation challenges.
Examine how NEP 2020 aims to promote vocational education and skill development, and its role in enhancing employability.
Critically analyze the role of digital education and technology in achieving the goals of NEP 2020, especially in remote areas.
Suggest strategies for effective collaboration between central and state governments, and other stakeholders, for the successful implementation of NEP 2020.
articles.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom setting, representing multidisciplinary learning.
articles.trivia: The Kothari Commission (1964-66) was instrumental in shaping India's first National Policy on Education.

articles.headline: Jal Jeevan Mission: Milestone Achievements in Rural Tap Water Connectivity
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Jal Shakti announces significant progress under the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM), with millions more rural households gaining access to tap water connections.
• The mission aims to provide safe and adequate drinking water through individual household tap connections by 2024.
• States with exemplary performance in achieving 'Har Ghar Jal' status are recognized, showcasing effective implementation strategies.
• Challenges remain in ensuring sustained water supply, water quality monitoring, and community participation in maintenance.
• Emphasis on sustainable water management, including source sustainability measures and greywater reuse, to ensure long-term impact.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
articles.source: Ministry of Jal Shakti / DD News
prelimsPoints: Jal Jeevan Mission was launched in August 2019.
The mission aims to provide FHTC to every rural household by 2024.
It falls under the Ministry of Jal Shakti.
The mission focuses on a community-driven approach.
Goa, Telangana, Haryana, Gujarat, Punjab, and Himachal Pradesh have achieved 100% 'Har Ghar Jal' status.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the success of the Jal Jeevan Mission in transforming rural access to safe drinking water and its implications for public health and women empowerment.
Discuss the challenges in achieving universal tap water connectivity, particularly concerning water quality, source sustainability, and equitable distribution.
Examine the role of community participation and local governance in the effective implementation and maintenance of water supply infrastructure under JJM.
Analyze how JJM contributes to India's achievement of Sustainable Development Goal 6 (Clean Water and Sanitation).
Suggest innovative approaches and policy interventions to ensure the long-term sustainability and resilience of rural water supply systems.
articles.imageDescription: A rural woman filling water from a tap connection at her home.
articles.trivia: Access to safe drinking water is recognized as a fundamental human right.

articles.headline: India Initiates Consultations for National AI Ethics and Governance Framework
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) commences nationwide consultations for developing a comprehensive national framework for Artificial Intelligence (AI) ethics and governance.
• The initiative aims to ensure responsible development and deployment of AI technologies, addressing concerns around bias, privacy, accountability, and job displacement.
• Emphasis on fostering innovation while safeguarding societal values and human rights in the age of AI.
• Discussions involve experts from academia, industry, civil society, and government to gather diverse perspectives.
• The framework is expected to provide guidelines for AI research, development, deployment, and public awareness programs.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
IT, Computers, Robotics, Nanotechnology, Bio-technology and issues relating to Intellectual Property Rights.
articles.source: MeitY / The Economic Times
prelimsPoints: AI refers to the simulation of human intelligence in machines programmed to think like humans.
MeitY is the nodal ministry for IT policy and digital initiatives in India.
AI ethics deals with the moral implications of AI development and use.
Concerns include algorithmic bias, privacy violations, and autonomous decision-making.
India aims to be a leader in AI for social good.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ethical challenges and societal implications of the rapid advancement and deployment of Artificial Intelligence technologies in India.
Analyze the need for a robust national AI ethics and governance framework in India, considering its potential benefits and risks.
Examine how such a framework can balance innovation in AI with the protection of individual rights, privacy, and prevention of algorithmic bias.
Evaluate the role of various stakeholders (government, industry, academia, civil society) in shaping and implementing effective AI governance policies.
Suggest key principles and regulatory mechanisms that should be incorporated into India's AI ethics and governance framework.
articles.imageDescription: An abstract representation of AI, showing interconnected nodes and human silhouettes.
articles.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956.

articles.headline: Government Launches Major Restoration Project for Ancient Temple Complex
articles.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) and the Ministry of Culture announce a multi-year, multi-crore project for the comprehensive restoration and preservation of a significant ancient temple complex in South India.
• The project involves structural conservation, chemical preservation, landscape restoration, and development of visitor amenities.
• Emphasis on using traditional techniques and materials, guided by archaeological principles, to maintain the site's authenticity.
• Challenges include managing environmental degradation, urban encroachment, and ensuring community engagement for sustainable preservation.
• The initiative aims to protect India's rich cultural heritage, promote tourism, and foster a sense of national pride.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
articles.source: Archaeological Survey of India / The Hindu
prelimsPoints: ASI is the premier organization for archaeological research and protection of cultural heritage in India.
India has numerous UNESCO World Heritage Sites, both cultural and natural.
Temple architecture in South India is broadly classified into Dravidian style.
Conservation efforts involve both structural repairs and chemical cleaning to prevent decay.
The Ancient Monuments and Archaeological Sites and Remains Act, 1958, governs protection of monuments.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of preserving India's ancient temple architecture as a vital part of its cultural heritage and national identity.
Analyze the challenges faced by organizations like ASI in the conservation and management of historical sites, including environmental, developmental, and financial constraints.
Evaluate the role of public-private partnerships and community involvement in ensuring the sustainable preservation of cultural heritage sites.
Examine how heritage tourism can be promoted responsibly to generate revenue while safeguarding the integrity of historical monuments.
Suggest policy measures to strengthen legal frameworks and institutional capacities for cultural heritage protection in India.
articles.imageDescription: A beautifully restored ancient temple gopuram.
articles.trivia: The Kailasa Temple at Ellora Caves is a monolithic structure carved out of a single rock.

articles.headline: PM-KISAN Scheme: Impact Assessment on Farmers' Income and Agricultural Productivity
articles.explainer:
• A new government report assesses the impact of the Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN) scheme on farmers' income, agricultural investment, and overall productivity.
• The report highlights positive effects on farmers' liquidity, reduction in debt burden, and increased adoption of modern farming practices.
• Challenges include identifying eligible beneficiaries, ensuring timely disbursements, and integrating with other agricultural support schemes.
• Discussions focus on refining the scheme's targeting mechanisms and exploring ways to enhance its effectiveness in boosting rural incomes.
• The scheme is a crucial component of the government's strategy to double farmers' income and promote agricultural welfare.
gsSubTopics: Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System- objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
Food processing and related industries in India- scope’ and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management.
articles.source: Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers' Welfare / PRS India
prelimsPoints: PM-KISAN scheme was launched in February 2019.
It provides an income support of Rs. 6,000 per year to all eligible farmer families in three equal installments.
The scheme is a Central Sector Scheme, fully funded by the Government of India.
The funds are directly transferred to the bank accounts of the beneficiaries (DBT mode).
Initially, it was for small and marginal farmers, but later extended to all farmer families.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the effectiveness of the PM-KISAN scheme as a direct income support mechanism for farmers and its contribution to doubling farmers' income.
Discuss the challenges in identifying eligible beneficiaries and ensuring the efficient and transparent delivery of benefits under PM-KISAN.
Analyze the broader impact of direct income support schemes on agricultural investment, productivity, and the welfare of the rural economy.
Examine how PM-KISAN can be better integrated with other agricultural policies and schemes to create a more comprehensive support system for farmers.
Suggest reforms to address the limitations of PM-KISAN and enhance its role in achieving sustainable agricultural development.
articles.imageDescription: A farmer working in a field, with a digital payment symbol overlaid.
articles.trivia: The concept of universal basic income for farmers has been discussed in various economic surveys.

articles.headline: NDMA Reviews National Disaster Management Preparedness Ahead of Monsoon Season
articles.explainer:
• The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) conducts a high-level review of national preparedness and response mechanisms for the upcoming monsoon season.
• Focus on early warning systems, evacuation protocols, resource mobilization, and inter-agency coordination for floods, landslides, and cyclones.
• Emphasizes strengthening community-level preparedness and capacity building, especially in vulnerable regions.
• Challenges include unpredictable weather patterns, rapid urbanization, and ensuring last-mile connectivity for relief efforts.
• Discussions include lessons learned from previous disasters and adopting technology for better forecasting and communication.
gsSubTopics: Disaster and disaster management.
articles.source: NDMA / UNI
prelimsPoints: NDMA is the apex body for disaster management in India, headed by the Prime Minister.
The Disaster Management Act was enacted in 2005.
NDRF is a specialized force for disaster response.
India is prone to various natural disasters like floods, droughts, cyclones, and earthquakes.
The Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015-2030) guides global efforts.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multi-hazard vulnerabilities of India and the institutional framework for disaster management at national and state levels.
Evaluate the effectiveness of India's early warning systems and disaster response mechanisms, citing recent examples.
Analyze the challenges in community-level disaster preparedness and suggest strategies for enhancing resilience and capacity building in vulnerable regions.
Examine the role of technology and innovation in improving disaster forecasting, communication, and relief operations.
Suggest policy recommendations for integrating disaster risk reduction into development planning to build a more disaster-resilient India.
articles.imageDescription: A rescue team conducting drills for flood response.
articles.trivia: The Indian Ocean is one of the six major tropical cyclone basins in the world.

articles.headline: Project Tiger and Lion: Government Plans Further Expansion of Protected Areas
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change announces plans for further expansion of protected areas under Project Tiger and Project Lion.
• The initiative aims to enhance habitat connectivity, mitigate human-wildlife conflict, and bolster conservation efforts for big cats.
• Highlights include successful breeding programs, anti-poaching measures, and community-based conservation models.
• Challenges involve increasing population pressure, habitat fragmentation, and ensuring ecological corridors for wildlife movement.
• Emphasis on scientific management, use of technology for monitoring, and international collaboration for species protection.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
articles.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change / Down To Earth
prelimsPoints: Project Tiger was launched in 1973.
India is home to over 70% of the world's tiger population.
Project Lion was launched in August 2020, focusing on Asiatic Lions in Gir.
The Wildlife Protection Act, 1972, provides legal framework for wildlife conservation.
Tigers are listed as 'Endangered' and Asiatic Lions as 'Endangered' on the IUCN Red List.
mainsPoints: Discuss the success and challenges of Project Tiger and Project Lion in conserving India's iconic big cat species.
Analyze the causes and consequences of human-wildlife conflict in India and suggest effective mitigation strategies.
Evaluate the role of protected areas, wildlife corridors, and community-based conservation initiatives in maintaining biodiversity.
Examine the impact of climate change and habitat fragmentation on wildlife populations and the measures needed for their long-term survival.
Suggest policy interventions to strengthen legal frameworks, enhance anti-poaching efforts, and promote sustainable ecotourism for wildlife conservation.
articles.imageDescription: A majestic tiger in its natural habitat, symbolizing conservation efforts.
articles.trivia: India has 54 Tiger Reserves as of 2023.

articles.headline: India Reiterates Call for Comprehensive Reforms of UN Security Council
articles.explainer:
• India once again reiterates its strong call for comprehensive reforms of the United Nations Security Council (UNSC), advocating for permanent membership.
• The demand is based on the argument that the current structure does not reflect contemporary geopolitical realities and the increased role of emerging powers.
• India highlights its contributions to UN peacekeeping, global development, and its status as the world's largest democracy.
• Challenges include resistance from existing P5 members and lack of consensus among UN member states on the modalities of reform.
• Discussions focus on the need for an equitable and representative UNSC to enhance its legitimacy and effectiveness in addressing global challenges.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
articles.source: Ministry of External Affairs / The Times of India
prelimsPoints: The UNSC has 15 members: 5 permanent (P5) and 10 non-permanent.
P5 members are China, France, Russia, the United Kingdom, and the United States.
Non-permanent members are elected for two-year terms by the General Assembly.
P5 members hold veto power.
India has served as a non-permanent member multiple times.
G4 nations (India, Brazil, Germany, Japan) mutually support each other's bids for permanent seats.
mainsPoints: Discuss the rationale behind India's persistent demand for a permanent seat in the UN Security Council and its implications for global governance.
Analyze the challenges and obstacles to achieving comprehensive reforms of the UNSC, considering the interests of various member states and blocs.
Evaluate India's contributions to global peace and security, economic development, and multilateralism, justifying its claim for an enhanced role in the UN.
Examine the different models and proposals for UNSC reform and the prospects for building consensus among UN member states.
Suggest a roadmap for India's diplomatic efforts to advance its aspirations for a permanent UNSC seat and strengthen multilateral institutions.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting the UN Security Council chamber with India's flag prominently displayed.
articles.trivia: The UN was established on October 24, 1945.

articles.headline: India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) Sees Further Expansion and Global Adoption
articles.explainer:
• India announces further expansion of its Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) stack, including Aadhaar, UPI, and DigiLocker, aiming for deeper penetration and wider service delivery.
• Discussions highlight the success of India's DPI model in fostering financial inclusion, digital payments, and efficient public services.
• Several developing nations express interest in adopting or adapting India's DPI framework for their own digital transformation journeys.
• Challenges include ensuring data privacy, cyber security, and bridging the digital literacy gap, especially in rural and marginalized communities.
• Emphasis on DPI as a model for equitable and inclusive growth, promoting innovation and interoperability.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
IT, Computers, Robotics, Nanotechnology, Bio-technology and issues relating to Intellectual Property Rights.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
articles.source: NITI Aayog / World Bank
prelimsPoints: DPI refers to shared digital systems like digital identity, payment infrastructure, and data exchange platforms.
Aadhaar is India's unique identification system.
UPI (Unified Payments Interface) is a real-time payment system developed by NPCI.
DigiLocker provides secure cloud-based access to documents and certificates.
India's DPI model is often referred to as 'India Stack'.
NPCI (National Payments Corporation of India) operates UPI.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative impact of India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) on financial inclusion, public service delivery, and economic growth.
Discuss the key components of India's DPI stack (Aadhaar, UPI, DigiLocker) and how they collectively contribute to a digital economy.
Examine the challenges associated with DPI, including data privacy concerns, cybersecurity risks, and the digital divide, and suggest mitigation strategies.
Evaluate the potential for global adoption of India's DPI model and its implications for digital cooperation among developing nations.
Suggest policy measures to further strengthen and expand India's DPI, ensuring equitable access and fostering innovation while maintaining security and trust.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic showing interconnected digital icons representing Aadhaar, UPI, and DigiLocker.
articles.trivia: UPI processed over 100 billion transactions in 2023.

articles.headline: Indian Startup Ecosystem Continues Robust Growth Amidst Funding Challenges
articles.explainer:
• A new report indicates continued robust growth in India's startup ecosystem, with a significant increase in new ventures and job creation.

articles.headline: Konkan Railway Celebrates Anniversary: A Testament to Engineering Prowess and Regional Connectivity
articles.explainer:
• Konkan Railway observes its anniversary, marking decades of its operational significance and impact on regional development.
• The railway line is celebrated as an engineering marvel, overcoming challenging terrain to connect India's western coast.
• It has significantly boosted economic activities, tourism, and connectivity for the Konkan region, reducing travel time.
• Discussions on ongoing modernization efforts, safety enhancements, and potential for further expansion or freight corridor integration.
• The Konkan Railway serves as a case study for large-scale infrastructure projects in difficult geographical conditions.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
articles.source: Konkan Railway Corporation Limited / Deccan Herald
prelimsPoints: Konkan Railway connects Maharashtra, Goa, and Karnataka along India's western coast.
It traverses through the rugged terrain of the Western Ghats.
The railway line became fully operational on April 24, 1993.
It is known for its numerous tunnels and bridges.
Konkan Railway Corporation Limited (KRCL) is a subsidiary of Indian Railways.

Analyze the challenges faced during the construction of the Konkan Railway, particularly in navigating the difficult terrain of the Western Ghats.
Evaluate the impact of the Konkan Railway on tourism, trade, and connectivity along India's western coast.
Examine how large-scale infrastructure projects like the Konkan Railway contribute to regional development and national integration.
Suggest measures for further modernizing and expanding railway infrastructure in challenging geographical areas of India.

articles.headline: G77 Plus China Calls for Equitable Climate Finance for Global South
articles.explainer:
• The Group of 77 (G77) and China bloc reiterates its demand for developed nations to fulfill their climate finance commitments to the Global South.
• The call comes ahead of major international climate summits, emphasizing the need for equitable burden-sharing in addressing climate change.
• Developing nations highlight their disproportionate vulnerability to climate impacts and the historical responsibility of developed countries.
• Discussions revolve around establishing a new collective quantified goal for climate finance, beyond the $100 billion per year target.
• Emphasis on ensuring accessible, adequate, and predictable financial support for adaptation, mitigation, and loss and damage in developing countries.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
articles.source: United Nations / The Wire
prelimsPoints: The G77 is a coalition of developing nations at the UN, representing their collective economic interests.
China, while not a formal member, often aligns with the G77 on climate issues.
Developed countries committed to mobilizing $100 billion per year in climate finance by 2020.
UNFCCC (United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change) is the primary international treaty on climate change.
The concept of 'Loss and Damage' refers to the unavoidable impacts of climate change.
mainsPoints: Discuss the concept of 'climate justice' and the historical responsibilities of developed nations concerning climate change and finance.
Analyze the persistent challenges in mobilizing and delivering adequate climate finance to developing countries, particularly for adaptation and loss and damage.
Evaluate the role of the G77 and China bloc in advocating for equitable climate action and resource allocation in international forums.
Examine the implications of a new collective quantified goal for climate finance and its potential impact on global climate cooperation.
Suggest mechanisms to ensure more predictable, accessible, and adequate climate finance for the Global South, promoting sustainable development.

WHO is the specialized agency of the UN for international public health.

India actively participates in various global health forums and initiatives.

Evaluate the effectiveness of international health regulations and pandemic preparedness frameworks, and suggest improvements based on recent experiences.

articles.headline: Urban Governance Reforms: Enhancing Efficiency and Citizen Participation in Smart Cities
articles.explainer:
• Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs outlines new urban governance reforms aimed at enhancing efficiency and citizen participation in Smart Cities.
• Focus on leveraging technology for urban planning, service delivery, and grievance redressal mechanisms.

highlights: National Panchayati Raj Day 2025 emphasizes digital transformation and women's leadership in rural governance, marking the 73rd Amendment's anniversary.
India's Q4 GDP projections indicate robust economic growth for FY25, driven by manufacturing and services, amidst global uncertainties.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the National Panchayati Raj Day, consider the following statements:
1. It is celebrated annually on 24th April to commemorate the passing of the 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992.
2. The Act added Part IX 'The Panchayats' to the Constitution, providing for a two-tier system of Panchayati Raj Institutions.
3. Article 243D mandates reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, and not less than one-third for women in Panchayats.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation:
Statement 1 is correct: National Panchayati Raj Day is celebrated on April 24th, marking the enforcement of the 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992, on April 24, 1993.
Statement 2 is incorrect: The Act provides for a three-tier system of Panchayati Raj Institutions (Gram Panchayat, Intermediate Panchayat, and District Panchayat), not a two-tier system, for states with a population exceeding 20 lakh.
Statement 3 is correct: Article 243D of the Constitution mandates reservation of seats for SCs, STs, and not less than one-third of the total seats for women in Panchayats.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements about India's National Green Hydrogen Mission are correct?
1. The mission was approved in January 2023.
2. It aims to achieve a green hydrogen production capacity of 5 Million Metric Tonnes (MMT) per annum by 2030.
3. Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water using electricity generated from fossil fuels.
4. The mission seeks to reduce fossil fuel imports and decarbonize industrial sectors.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation:
Statement 1 is correct: The National Green Hydrogen Mission was approved by the Union Cabinet in January 2023.
Statement 2 is correct: One of the key targets of the mission is to achieve a green hydrogen production capacity of at least 5 Million Metric Tonnes (MMT) per annum by 2030.
Statement 3 is incorrect: Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water using renewable electricity (e.g., from solar or wind), not fossil fuels. Hydrogen produced using fossil fuels is typically referred to as grey or blue hydrogen.
Statement 4 is correct: The mission's objectives include reducing dependence on fossil fuel imports and contributing to the decarbonization of various sectors.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Jal Jeevan Mission:
1. It was launched in August 2019 with the aim to provide safe and adequate drinking water through individual household tap connections by 2024.
2. The mission is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme, with funding shared between the Centre and States.
3. It emphasizes a community-driven approach, with Gram Panchayats playing a key role in planning and implementation.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation:
Statement 1 is correct: The Jal Jeevan Mission was launched in August 2019 with the stated objective of providing Functional Household Tap Connections (FHTC) to every rural household by 2024.
Statement 2 is incorrect: The Jal Jeevan Mission is not a Centrally Sponsored Scheme; it is a Central Sector Scheme, meaning it is fully funded by the Government of India for Union Territories and Himalayan/North-Eastern states, and with a 50:50 sharing pattern for other states.
Statement 3 is correct: The mission adopts a community-driven approach, where local communities, particularly Gram Panchayats, are actively involved in the planning, implementation, management, operation, and maintenance of the water supply systems.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) stack, often referred to as 'India Stack'?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Aadhaar (digital identity), UPI (payment system), and DigiLocker (data exchange) are all core components of India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) stack, often referred to as 'India Stack'. The National Optical Fibre Network (NOFN), now BharatNet, is a physical infrastructure project for broadband connectivity, which supports DPI but is not considered a direct component of the 'India Stack' software layer itself. India Stack primarily comprises a set of APIs that allows governments, businesses, startups and developers to utilise a unique digital infrastructure to solve problems.

prelimsMocks.question:
Regarding the UN Security Council (UNSC) and India's aspirations, consider the following:
1. The UNSC has 15 members, comprising 5 permanent members and 10 non-permanent members.
2. The 5 permanent members (P5) possess veto power.
3. India is a member of the G4 nations, which collectively advocate for permanent seats in the UNSC.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation:
Statement 1 is correct: The UN Security Council consists of 15 members: five permanent members (China, France, Russia, the United Kingdom, and the United States) and ten non-permanent members elected for two-year terms.
Statement 2 is correct: The five permanent members (P5) hold the power of veto, allowing them to block any substantive resolution.
Statement 3 is correct: India is indeed part of the G4 nations (Brazil, Germany, India, and Japan), which are four countries that support each other's bids for permanent seats on the UN Security Council.

mainsMocks.question: Critically analyze the impact of the 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act on democratic decentralization in India. Discuss the persistent challenges faced by Panchayati Raj Institutions in achieving true self-governance and suggest measures for their empowerment. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the 73rd Amendment and its significance. Discuss successes like increased representation (especially for women/SC/ST) and bringing governance closer to people. Then, detail challenges such as inadequate financial autonomy, limited devolution of powers/functions, bureaucratic interference, lack of capacity building, and ineffective Gram Sabhas. Conclude with concrete suggestions for empowerment, like strengthening State Finance Commissions, ensuring functional devolution, leveraging technology, and enhancing community participation.

mainsMocks.question: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission is a strategic initiative for both energy security and climate action. Discuss the potential benefits of green hydrogen for India and analyze the key technological and economic challenges in scaling up its production and adoption. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment; Infrastructure: Energy; Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
mainsMocks.approach: Begin by defining green hydrogen and stating the mission's objectives. Highlight benefits such as reduced fossil fuel imports, decarbonization of hard-to-abate sectors, job creation, and export potential. Then, delve into challenges like high capital costs for electrolysers, need for abundant renewable energy, infrastructure for storage/transport, R&D for efficiency, and demand creation. Conclude with policy recommendations for overcoming these hurdles and achieving mission targets.

mainsMocks.question: India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) has been lauded globally for fostering financial inclusion and efficient public service delivery. Examine the key components of India's DPI stack and critically evaluate its impact on socio-economic development. What are the associated risks, and how can they be mitigated? (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life; IT, Computers; Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce DPI and 'India Stack' (Aadhaar, UPI, DigiLocker). Explain how each component contributes to digital identity, payments, and data exchange. Discuss positive impacts: financial inclusion (Jan Dhan, DBT), ease of doing business, reduced corruption, innovation, and efficient service delivery. Address risks: data privacy, cybersecurity, digital divide, exclusion of digitally illiterate, and potential for surveillance. Conclude with mitigation strategies: robust data protection laws, cybersecurity frameworks, digital literacy programs, and inclusive design.

India aims to achieve 5 MMT per annum green hydrogen production capacity by 2030 under its National Green Hydrogen Mission.
ISRO's Gaganyaan mission aims to send a three-member crew to an orbit of 400 km for a 3-day mission.

The G4 nations (India, Brazil, Germany, Japan) advocate for permanent seats in the UN Security Council.
The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) is the premier organization for archaeological research and heritage protection in India.

GS1.headline: Government Launches Major Restoration Project for Ancient Temple Complex
GS1.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) and the Ministry of Culture announce a multi-year, multi-crore project for the comprehensive restoration and preservation of a significant ancient temple complex in South India.
• The project involves structural conservation, chemical preservation, landscape restoration, and development of visitor amenities.
• Emphasis on using traditional techniques and materials, guided by archaeological principles, to maintain the site's authenticity.
• Challenges include managing environmental degradation, urban encroachment, and ensuring community engagement for sustainable preservation.
• The initiative aims to protect India's rich cultural heritage, promote tourism, and foster a sense of national pride.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
GS1.source: Archaeological Survey of India / The Hindu
prelimsPoints: ASI is the premier organization for archaeological research and protection of cultural heritage in India.
India has numerous UNESCO World Heritage Sites, both cultural and natural.
Temple architecture in South India is broadly classified into Dravidian style.
Conservation efforts involve both structural repairs and chemical cleaning to prevent decay.
The Ancient Monuments and Archaeological Sites and Remains Act, 1958, governs protection of monuments.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of preserving India's ancient temple architecture as a vital part of its cultural heritage and national identity.
Analyze the challenges faced by organizations like ASI in the conservation and management of historical sites, including environmental, developmental, and financial constraints.
Evaluate the role of public-private partnerships and community involvement in ensuring the sustainable preservation of cultural heritage sites.
Examine how heritage tourism can be promoted responsibly to generate revenue while safeguarding the integrity of historical monuments.
Suggest policy measures to strengthen legal frameworks and institutional capacities for cultural heritage protection in India.
GS1.imageDescription: A beautifully restored ancient temple gopuram.
GS1.trivia: The Kailasa Temple at Ellora Caves is a monolithic structure carved out of a single rock.

GS2.headline: National Panchayati Raj Day 2025: Focus on Digital Transformation and Women's Leadership
GS2.explainer:
• India celebrates National Panchayati Raj Day, commemorating the 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act coming into force on April 24, 1993.
• This year's theme emphasizes 'Digital Panchayats: Empowering Rural India' and enhancing women's participation in local governance.
• Government highlights progress in e-governance initiatives like eGramSwaraj and online services, aiming for greater transparency and efficiency.
• Discussions focus on challenges such as digital literacy gaps, infrastructure availability, and financial autonomy for PRIs.
• Emphasis on strengthening the role of women elected representatives and promoting grassroots leadership for inclusive development.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States
Devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
Role of women and women's organization
GS2.source: Ministry of Panchayati Raj / The Hindu
prelimsPoints: National Panchayati Raj Day is observed annually on April 24.
The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992, came into force on April 24, 1993.
The Act added Part IX 'The Panchayats' to the Constitution.
It mandates the establishment of a three-tier Panchayati Raj system in states with a population exceeding 20 lakh.
Article 243D provides for reservation of seats for SCs, STs, and not less than one-third for women.
eGramSwaraj is a simplified work-based accounting application for Panchayati Raj.
First Panchayati Raj system was inaugurated in Nagaur, Rajasthan, on Oct 2, 1959.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the impact of the 73rd Amendment on democratic decentralization in India, focusing on its successes and persistent challenges.
Discuss how digital initiatives like eGramSwaraj can enhance transparency and efficiency in Panchayati Raj Institutions, while also addressing the digital divide in rural areas.
Analyze the role of women in local governance and suggest measures to further strengthen their leadership and decision-making capacity within PRIs.
Examine the financial autonomy of Panchayats and the recommendations of State Finance Commissions in strengthening grassroots democracy.
Discuss the significance of Gram Sabha in fostering participatory democracy and how its effectiveness can be improved.
GS2.imageDescription: A graphic depicting the three-tier Panchayati Raj system with digital elements and women leaders.
GS2.trivia: The concept of 'Gram Swaraj' was envisioned by Mahatma Gandhi, emphasizing self-sufficient village republics.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Bench to Hear Petitions on Federal Fiscal Relations and Resource Devolution
GS2.explainer:
• A Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court is set to hear crucial petitions concerning the principles of federal fiscal relations and the devolution of resources between the Union and states.
• The petitions challenge certain aspects of the Goods and Services Tax (GST) framework and the methodology of financial transfers.
• States argue for greater fiscal autonomy and a larger share of central taxes, citing increasing expenditure responsibilities.
• The Union government emphasizes the need for a unified economic framework and equitable distribution across states.
• The judgment is expected to have significant implications for cooperative federalism and the financial health of states.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States
Issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure
Devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
prelimsPoints: GST is a destination-based consumption tax.
The GST Council is a constitutional body (Article 279A) that makes recommendations on GST issues.
The Finance Commission (Article 280) recommends the distribution of tax revenues between the Union and states.
Cooperative federalism emphasizes cooperation between different levels of government.
Article 268 to 281 in Part XII of the Constitution deals with financial relations between Centre and states.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving nature of fiscal federalism in India, particularly in the post-GST era, and the challenges faced by states in maintaining fiscal autonomy.
Analyze the role of the Supreme Court in adjudicating disputes related to Centre-State financial relations and its impact on cooperative federalism.
Critically evaluate the recommendations of the latest Finance Commission regarding vertical and horizontal devolution of resources.
Examine the arguments for and against greater fiscal autonomy for states, considering both economic efficiency and equity.
Suggest reforms to strengthen the federal fiscal framework in India, ensuring both stability for the Union and adequate resources for states.
GS2.imageDescription: Illustration of the Supreme Court building with scales representing federal balance.
GS2.trivia: India's federal system is often described as 'quasi-federal' due to a strong central bias.

GS2.headline: India-UK Free Trade Agreement Negotiations Enter Final Stages
GS2.explainer:
• Negotiations for the India-UK Free Trade Agreement (FTA) are reported to be in their final stages, with both sides optimistic about an imminent conclusion.
• The FTA aims to significantly boost bilateral trade in goods and services, reduce tariffs, and open market access for businesses.
• Key sticking points reportedly include intellectual property rights, rules of origin, and services sector liberalization.
• A successful FTA is expected to enhance economic cooperation, create jobs, and foster greater investment flows between the two nations.
• The agreement is seen as a crucial step for India's 'Act East' policy and the UK's post-Brexit trade strategy.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India's interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India's interests, Indian diaspora.
GS2.source: Ministry of Commerce and Industry / Reuters
prelimsPoints: An FTA is a pact between two or more countries to reduce barriers to imports and exports among them.
India is negotiating FTAs with several countries/blocs, including the UK, EU, and Canada.
The UK is a significant trading partner for India, especially after Brexit.
Key sectors of interest for India include textiles, pharmaceuticals, and IT services.
Services constitute a large portion of bilateral trade between India and the UK.
mainsPoints: Analyze the potential economic benefits and challenges for India arising from a comprehensive Free Trade Agreement with the United Kingdom.
Discuss how the India-UK FTA aligns with India's broader trade policy objectives and its 'Act East' strategy.
Examine the key areas of contention in FTA negotiations and how they reflect the differing economic priorities of India and the UK.
Evaluate the impact of such FTAs on domestic industries and suggest measures to mitigate any adverse effects.
Discuss the role of trade agreements in strengthening India's position in the global economic order.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and the United Kingdom superimposed over a handshake icon.
GS2.trivia: India is the world's fifth-largest economy, and the UK is the sixth-largest.

GS2.headline: National Education Policy 2020: Review of Implementation Progress and Future Roadmap
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education conducts a comprehensive review of the implementation progress of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 across states and institutions.
• Highlights include advancements in curriculum reform, vocational education integration, and digital learning initiatives.
• Challenges persist in teacher training, infrastructure development, equitable access, and ensuring smooth transition across different education levels.
• Discussions focus on accelerating the adoption of NEP recommendations, particularly in higher education and early childhood care.
• Emphasis on stakeholder collaboration, including state governments, educational institutions, and civil society, for successful policy execution.
gsSubTopics: Development processes and the development industry —the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.

prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education.
It proposes a new 5+3+3+4 curricular structure.
Focus on multidisciplinary education and flexibility in course choices.
The policy aims for universalization of Early Childhood Care and Education (ECCE) by 2030.
It recommends increasing public investment in education to 6% of GDP.

Examine how NEP 2020 aims to promote vocational education and skill development, and its role in enhancing employability.
Critically analyze the role of digital education and technology in achieving the goals of NEP 2020, especially in remote areas.
Suggest strategies for effective collaboration between central and state governments, and other stakeholders, for the successful implementation of NEP 2020.

GS2.trivia: The Kothari Commission (1964-66) was instrumental in shaping India's first National Policy on Education.

GS2.headline: Jal Jeevan Mission: Milestone Achievements in Rural Tap Water Connectivity
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Jal Shakti announces significant progress under the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM), with millions more rural households gaining access to tap water connections.
• The mission aims to provide safe and adequate drinking water through individual household tap connections by 2024.
• States with exemplary performance in achieving 'Har Ghar Jal' status are recognized, showcasing effective implementation strategies.
• Challenges remain in ensuring sustained water supply, water quality monitoring, and community participation in maintenance.
• Emphasis on sustainable water management, including source sustainability measures and greywater reuse, to ensure long-term impact.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.

The mission aims to provide FHTC to every rural household by 2024.

Goa, Telangana, Haryana, Gujarat, Punjab, and Himachal Pradesh have achieved 100% 'Har Ghar Jal' status.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the success of the Jal Jeevan Mission in transforming rural access to safe drinking water and its implications for public health and women empowerment.
Discuss the challenges in achieving universal tap water connectivity, particularly concerning water quality, source sustainability, and equitable distribution.
